summary: Gruber provides a detailed technical analysis of Apple's compliance with the EU's DMA mandate requiring iPhones to share Wi-Fi network credentials with third-party accessories. He argues that reporting from Numerama and others inaccurately characterized Apple as spitefully removing Wi-Fi sync between iPhone and Apple Watch, when Apple actually chose a middle ground: limiting historical Wi-Fi network sync to Apple Watch while creating an entirely new Wi-Fi Infrastructure API framework for third-party devices in the EU. Gruber contends the EC's mandate is incoherent because it demands both user privacy protection and broad third-party access to sensitive Wi-Fi data, without allowing Apple to enforce privacy requirements on those third parties. He frames this as a clarifying example of how the DMA prioritizes developers over users, noting that Apple engineered significant new APIs solely for EU compliance that may never be used — much like the BrowserEngineKit framework that no browser has adopted in two years.
key_points: Apple is not removing Wi-Fi sync between iPhone and Apple Watch in the EU — it's limiting historical network sync while creating new public APIs (Wi-Fi Infrastructure framework) for third-party accessories
The EU mandate prevents Apple from requiring third-party device makers to keep Wi-Fi network data on-device, creating a genuine privacy risk that Apple treats as its responsibility to mitigate

Apple's compliance follows a middle ground: new Apple Watches in the EU won't get historical Wi-Fi networks, but will still sync future networks — and third-party devices get equivalent access via new APIs

The DMA fundamentally inverts Apple's priority order (Apple first, users second, developers third) to privilege developers first, and this Wi-Fi case illustrates how that harms users without clear benefit
Meta is likely the driving force behind this specific interoperability mandate, and it remains unclear whether any third-party company will actually use the EU-exclusive Wi-Fi Infrastructure APIs
money_quotes: Apple clearly thinks it's a bad idea to share any Wi-Fi information at all with third-party devices, but they've created an entire new framework for use within the EU to allow it, just so they can continue syncing any Wi-Fi network information at all with Apple Watch.

The EC insists that Apple should protect users' privacy and security, while also insisting that Apple grant access to third-party apps and devices that can potentially compromise users' privacy and security.
Apple sees the DMA as mandating, privacy-wise, that they create something akin to cars that don't require keys, trusting EU law to keep them from being stolen.

The 'it should be up to the user' take benefits informed, technically savvy users. The 'it shouldn't be possible' take benefits uninformed, un-savvy users — users who in many cases have decided that they simply trust Apple.
It'd be pretty weird if Apple limited itself, when designing and engineering features that integrate across its own devices, to what it would allow third-party developers to do.
Apple doesn't care about €20 million. They fart bigger than that. They do care about clarifying who the DMA prioritizes first, and that it's not users.
These frameworks might be the largest set of APIs ever created that never get used.
But don't make the mistake of thinking that Apple is complying the least bit spitefully with regard to this mandate.
key_insight: Apple's DMA compliance on Wi-Fi network sharing is not spiteful removal of features but a genuine engineering effort to balance an incoherent mandate that simultaneously demands user privacy protection and broad third-party access to sensitive data.
